{{Chess Opening Theory/Position|= |Queen's Indian Defence| |rd|nd|bd|qd|kd|bd| |rd|= |pd| |pd|pd| |pd|pd|pd|= | |pd| | |pd|nd| | |= | | | | | | | | |= | | |pl|pl| | | | |= | | | | | |nl| | |= |pl|pl| | |pl|pl|pl|pl|= |rl|nl|bl|ql|kl|bl| |rl|= |moves=1.d4 Nf6 2. c4 e6 3.Nf3 b6 |eco=[[Chess/ECOE|E12-E19]] |parent=[[Chess/Indian Defence|Indian Defence]] }} ==Queen's Indian Defence== The Queen's Indian Defence is a popular and fairly modern opening whereby black quickly develops his queenside bishop, either by fianchettoing it on b7 to contest the long diagonal, or on a6 to put pressure on white's c-pawn. It has been employed regularly by a number of top players including former world champions like Anatoly Karpov and Viswanathan Anand. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}} '''1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 e6 3.
